model_narrative: A pipe network must be laid out to collect production from a set of nodes and route it to a single port. For each directed connection between two nodes the model decides whether to lay a baseline pipe and, on top of that baseline, whether to add a single capacity upgrade. It also decides how much flow runs along each connection. The goal is to choose the cheapest set of pipes and upgrades that can carry every node's production to the port, where the cost of any pipe scales with the distance it spans.

sets.name: n
sets.doc: nodes in the network

sets.name: k
sets.doc: pipe types; type 2 is the baseline and the rest are larger

sets.name: kk
sets.doc: upgrade pipe types, that is the pipe types larger than the baseline

sets.name: regnode
sets.doc: flow-conserving nodes, that is every node except the port

sets.name: nw
sets.doc: nodes that may originate a pipe

sets.name: arc
sets.doc: directed arcs (i, j); both orientations of every physical edge are present, but no arc leaves the port

params.name: cap1
params.doc: the baseline pipe capacity that any built pipe provides

params.name: pipecost1
params.doc: the baseline pipe cost per unit distance

params.name: cap_adj
params.doc: incremental flow capacity of an upgrade type, measured as the extra capacity it adds over the baseline

params.name: pipecost_adj
params.doc: incremental cost per unit distance of an upgrade type, measured as the extra cost it adds over the baseline

params.name: p
params.doc: production at a node; a node with zero production is a pass-through node, a node with positive production is a source

vars.name: b
vars.doc: 1 if a baseline pipe is laid on the arc, else 0

vars.name: bk
vars.doc: 1 if the given upgrade type is added on top of the baseline pipe on the arc, else 0; only upgrade types in kk are used

vars.name: f
vars.doc: flow sent along the arc

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: total installation cost of the chosen network

constraints.name: pipecost1
constraints.intent: Define the total installation cost as the sum over every arc of the arc's length multiplied by the cost of whatever is built on it, where a built baseline pipe contributes its baseline cost and each added upgrade contributes its own incremental cost.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each pass-through node that has no production, allow at most one baseline pipe to leave the node. Skip this requirement at any node that has production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each node that has production, require exactly one baseline pipe to leave the node so its output has a single starting direction. Skip this requirement at any node with no production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: regnode:
constraints.intent: At every flow-conserving node, the node's own production must equal the total flow leaving it minus the total flow entering it, so production plus whatever arrives is exactly what departs. Skip this at the port, which simply absorbs all the collected production.

constraints.name: cap1
constraints.intent: On every arc, the capacity installed there must be enough to carry the flow on that arc, where the installed capacity is the baseline capacity of a built pipe plus the extra capacity contributed by any upgrade added to it.

constraints.name: bk[i,
constraints.intent: On every arc, an upgrade may only be added where a baseline pipe is actually built, and since only one upgrade can ever be present the total number of upgrades on the arc can be at most one when a baseline pipe is laid and none otherwise.

query: Engineering is pitching a routing principle: any time we feed oil into the node-3 collector, we should also commit to the onward leg that carries it forward toward the port, so flow never dead-ends at that collector. Build that linkage in and walk me through what the network ends up costing.

query: There's a proposal on the table to hold the build to a much leaner footprint - no more than roughly 22.5 percent of all the candidate corridors in the network should carry pipe. Apply that ceiling on the total share of corridors used and tell me the resulting construction cost.
expected_pyomo: model.cap_eastern = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc) <= 0.225*len(model.arc))

query: We're looking at a redundancy-avoidance rule for the junctions: at every node, the number of inbound pipes we build should stay at or below half of all the corridors that could physically feed that node. Spread the inflow that way across the whole network and walk me through the new total cost.
expected_pyomo:
def _spread(model, n): return sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc if a[1] == n) <= 0.5*len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) if len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) >= 2 else Constraint.Skip
model.spread_inflow = Constraint(model.n, rule=_spread)

query: The board is pitching a combined scenario: keep the overall build lean by holding pipe to at most roughly 22.5 percent of all candidate corridors, and at the same time guarantee that whenever we feed the node-3 collector we also lay the onward leg out of it. Run both of those together and tell me the resulting cost.
expected_pyomo:
model.cap_total = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc) <= 0.225*len(model.arc))
model.feed_onward = Constraint(expr=model.b['3', '4'] >= model.b['1', '3'])

expected_pyomo: model.cap_segments = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc) <= 30)

query: The plan keeps building out a lot of corridors - it feels like we're laying pipe on far more of the network than we should need. Couldn't we hold the build to something like a fifth of all the candidate corridors, no more than about 22 percent of them carrying pipe? What's keeping the model from making that move?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_routes = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc) <= 0.22*len(model.arc))

query: At some of the junctions the plan lights up every single corridor that could feed the node, which looks like it's saturating those collectors completely. Wouldn't it be cleaner to leave at least one feasible inbound corridor unused at every node rather than maxing them all out? What's pulling the design toward filling them up?
expected_pyomo:
def _nofull(model, n): return sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc if a[1] == n) <= len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) - 1 if len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) >= 2 else Constraint.Skip
model.no_full_inflow = Constraint(model.n, rule=_nofull)

query: Several of the collector nodes look heavily over-fed - the plan piles a lot of inbound pipes onto them. Shouldn't each node's inbound pipe count stay under roughly half of the corridors that could feed it, so no single junction gets overloaded? What's the model seeing that I'm not?
expected_pyomo:
def _thin(model, n): return sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc if a[1] == n) <= 0.46*len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) if len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) >= 2 else Constraint.Skip
model.thin_inflow = Constraint(model.n, rule=_thin)

model_narrative: A pipe network must be laid out to collect production from a set of nodes and route it to a single port. For each directed connection between two nodes the model decides whether to lay a baseline pipe and, on top of that baseline, whether to add a single capacity upgrade. It also decides how much flow runs along each connection. The goal is to choose the cheapest set of pipes and upgrades that can carry every node's production to the port, where the cost of any pipe scales with the distance it spans.

sets.name: n
sets.doc: nodes in the network

sets.name: k
sets.doc: pipe types; type 2 is the baseline and the rest are larger

sets.name: kk
sets.doc: upgrade pipe types, that is the pipe types larger than the baseline

sets.name: regnode
sets.doc: flow-conserving nodes, that is every node except the port

sets.name: nw
sets.doc: nodes that may originate a pipe

sets.name: arc
sets.doc: directed arcs (i, j); both orientations of every physical edge are present, but no arc leaves the port

params.name: cap1
params.doc: the baseline pipe capacity that any built pipe provides

params.name: pipecost1
params.doc: the baseline pipe cost per unit distance

params.name: cap_adj
params.doc: incremental flow capacity of an upgrade type, measured as the extra capacity it adds over the baseline

params.name: pipecost_adj
params.doc: incremental cost per unit distance of an upgrade type, measured as the extra cost it adds over the baseline

params.name: p
params.doc: production at a node; a node with zero production is a pass-through node, a node with positive production is a source

vars.name: b
vars.doc: 1 if a baseline pipe is laid on the arc, else 0

vars.name: bk
vars.doc: 1 if the given upgrade type is added on top of the baseline pipe on the arc, else 0; only upgrade types in kk are used

vars.name: f
vars.doc: flow sent along the arc

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: total installation cost of the chosen network

constraints.name: pipecost1
constraints.intent: Define the total installation cost as the sum over every arc of the arc's length multiplied by the cost of whatever is built on it, where a built baseline pipe contributes its baseline cost and each added upgrade contributes its own incremental cost.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each pass-through node that has no production, allow at most one baseline pipe to leave the node. Skip this requirement at any node that has production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each node that has production, require exactly one baseline pipe to leave the node so its output has a single starting direction. Skip this requirement at any node with no production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: regnode:
constraints.intent: At every flow-conserving node, the node's own production must equal the total flow leaving it minus the total flow entering it, so production plus whatever arrives is exactly what departs. Skip this at the port, which simply absorbs all the collected production.

constraints.name: cap1
constraints.intent: On every arc, the capacity installed there must be enough to carry the flow on that arc, where the installed capacity is the baseline capacity of a built pipe plus the extra capacity contributed by any upgrade added to it.

constraints.name: bk[i,
constraints.intent: On every arc, an upgrade may only be added where a baseline pipe is actually built, and since only one upgrade can ever be present the total number of upgrades on the arc can be at most one when a baseline pipe is laid and none otherwise.

query: Two things about the plan bother me at once: the collector junctions get every feasible inbound corridor lit up, and at the node-3 collector we feed oil in without committing the onward leg that moves it forward. Couldn't we both leave at least one inbound corridor spare at every node and require that onward leg whenever node 3 is fed? What's the tradeoff that's keeping the design from doing both?
expected_pyomo:
def _nofull(model, n): return sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc if a[1] == n) <= len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) - 1 if len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) >= 2 else Constraint.Skip
model.no_full_inflow = Constraint(model.n, rule=_nofull)
model.feed_onward = Constraint(expr=model.b['3', '4'] >= model.b['1', '3'])

query: Here's what's nagging me about the layout: the collectors get over-fed - more than half their feasible inbound corridors built - and on top of that node 3 gets oil pushed into it with no committed leg carrying it onward. Wouldn't it be more sensible to hold each node's inbound pipes to at most half its feasible corridors AND insist the onward leg out of node 3 exists whenever we feed it? What's pulling the plan the other way?
expected_pyomo:
def _spread(model, n): return sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc if a[1] == n) <= 0.5*len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) if len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) >= 2 else Constraint.Skip
model.spread_inflow = Constraint(model.n, rule=_spread)
model.feed_onward = Constraint(expr=model.b['3', '4'] >= model.b['1', '3'])
